does: This paper presents RUBEN, an interactive tool that discovers minimal rule-based explanations for retrieval-augmented LLM systems by identifying compact sets of rules that capture the model's decision-making patterns. The work employs novel pruning strategies to efficiently extract these minimal rules from model behavior, and demonstrates how such rules can be applied to test LLM safety mechanisms, including evaluating the robustness of safety training and the success of adversarial prompt injection attacks. The core contribution lies in making retrieval-augmented systems more interpretable and actionable through rule discovery that goes beyond standard explanation methods.
connects: This work builds on prior research in neural network interpretability and rule extraction, extending these ideas to the challenging setting of retrieval-augmented LLMs which combine dense retrieval with generation. It responds to growing concerns about unexplainability in large language models and safety vulnerabilities, connecting to both mechanistic interpretability work that seeks to reverse-engineer model behavior and adversarial robustness research that tests model resilience. The paper also draws on rule-learning literature and connects to recent work on understanding and auditing retrieval-augmented systems, where the interaction between retrieved context and generation creates new interpretability challenges not present in purely generative models.
enables: RUBEN's rule extraction capability opens pathways for more systematic safety auditing of retrieval-augmented systems, enabling practitioners to identify exploitable patterns before deployment. The minimal rule sets could support red-teaming efforts by revealing which retrieved documents or input patterns consistently lead to unsafe outputs, facilitating targeted improvements to safety training. More broadly, the approach suggests future work on using discovered rules for model debugging, prompt engineering refinement, and building safer retrieval indices. The interactive nature of the tool also enables human-in-the-loop model improvement and could support regulatory compliance by providing auditable explanations for model decisions in sensitive applications.
